channel.title: Logic Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Logic Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: False
item.description: False is the semantic property a proposition has when what it claims to be the case is not the case. Aristotle’s formulation in Metaphysics Γ — “to say of what is that it is not, or of what is not that it is, is false” — gives the classical sense, and remains the reference point against which non-classical accounts position themselves. Falsity is not merely the absence of truth; it is the positive ascription of a contrary, and on most accounts it stands in a structured relation to the operations of negation and refutation.

item.title: True
item.description: True is the semantic property a proposition has when what it claims to be the case is the case. Aristotle’s formulation in Metaphysics Γ — “to say of what is that it is, and of what is not that it is not, is true” — remains the touchstone: truth is a relation between what is claimed and what is so. The proposition “snow is white” is true when snow is white. The disagreements among the major theories of truth concern not this minimal claim but what the relation amounts to, what its bearers are, and whether it admits of degrees, gaps, or gluts.
